did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the YOLOv11 deep learning algorithm (a computer vision object detection model) to classify four types of skin diseases (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Melanoma) using a Kaggle dataset of 2,000 images. The study achieved an overall accuracy of 79% on the validation set.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work implementing the YOLOv11 algorithm to classify skin diseases using a Kaggle dataset of 2,000 images. It details specific methodology (data preprocessing, augmentation, training on Google Colab with T4 GPU for 100 epochs) and reports new empirical results (79% accuracy, precision/recall/F1 scores for specific classes), distinguishing it from a review or survey.

title: CLASSIFICATION OF SKIN DISEASES USING YOLOV11
brief_summary: This study implements the YOLOv11 deep learning algorithm to classify four types of skin diseases: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Melanoma. The model was trained and evaluated using a Kaggle dataset comprising 2,000 images, which were resized to 640×640 pixels and augmented to enhance robustness. The data was split into training (85%), validation (10%), and testing (5%) sets, with training conducted on a Google Colab T4 GPU for 100 epochs. The model achieved an overall accuracy of 79% on the validation set, with strong performance for Actinic Keratosis (precision=0.92, recall=0.92) but lower recall for Melanoma (0.59). Aggregate metrics showed a precision of 0.80, recall of 0.73, and F1-score of 0.76, demonstrating reliable detection despite class imbalance issues. The authors conclude that YOLOv11 outperforms previous Image Processing and ANN methods (72% accuracy) but note limitations regarding dataset size and image variability.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four primary classes in the Kaggle dataset (500 images). Classified as a pre-cancerous lesion. Achieved precision=0.92, recall=0.92, F1=0.92 on the validation set.

One of the four primary classes in the Kaggle dataset (500 images). Classified as a dangerous skin cancer. Achieved precision=0.73, recall=0.59, F1=0.66 on the validation set. Performance noted as lower likely due to class imbalance.

all_task_types.target: Four discrete skin disease categories: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Melanoma.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify four types of skin diseases' using YOLOv11. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels for images from a Kaggle dataset. Evaluation metrics include Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score for these specific classes. Although YOLO is an object detection algorithm, the study treats the task as multi-class classification of the entire image content into one of four disease categories, as evidenced by the reporting of class-wise precision/recall and overall accuracy rather than bounding box coordinates or segmentation masks.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset obtained from the Kaggle platform containing images of skin diseases. The dataset was labeled using Roboflow and preprocessed by resizing images to 640x640 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the YOLOv11 model (85% of data, 1700 images) with data augmentation techniques including flipping, rotation, shear, and brightness adjustments.
use.validation: Used for validation/testing to evaluate model performance (10% of data, 200 images).
use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate model performance (5% of data, 100 images). Note: The paper reports results primarily on the validation set as the 'testing' phase in the context of the final evaluation metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset (Validation.notes: The model outperformed previous Image Processing and ANN methods (which achieved 72% accuracy). Performance was strong for Actinic Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma but lower for Melanoma, likely due to class imbalance. The confusion matrix showed high diagonal intensity, indicating good generalization, though some mislabeling occurred on images with low contrast or very small lesions.

architecture_details.description: Iterasi terbaru dalam seri YOLO, dibangun di atas fondasi YOLOv8. Mengintegrasikan teknik ekstraksi fitur canggih untuk computer vision, mendeteksi objek secara real-time dengan pendekatan regresi tunggal. Diperkenalkan pada konferensi YOLOVision 2024 (YV24).

training_details.hardware: Google Colab T4 GPU, 2 CPUs, 12.7 GB RAM

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation on the training set including Flip, 90° Rotate, Rotation, Shear, and Brightness adjustments

conclusions: The YOLOv11 algorithm achieved an overall accuracy of 79% on the validation set for classifying four skin diseases (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevus, and Melanoma), outperforming previous Image Processing and ANN methods which achieved 72% accuracy.

The model demonstrated strong performance for Actinic Keratosis (precision=0.92, recall=0.92, F1=0.92) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (precision=0.96, recall=0.96, F1=0.96), but lower performance for Melanoma (recall=0.59) and Melanocytic Nevus (precision=0.50, recall=0.67).
Aggregate evaluation metrics showed a precision of 0.80, recall of 0.73, and F1-score of 0.76, indicating reliable detection despite uneven performance across disease types.
The study acknowledges limitations including a limited dataset size (2,000 images) affecting model generalization, variability in image quality and lighting, and bias toward certain classes.
The authors recommend using larger datasets, such as ISIC, and exploring various versions of YOLOv11 in future research to improve model reliability and accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, balanced Kaggle dataset of only 2,000 images (500 per class), which limits model generalization and external validity.

There is a discrepancy in the literature review section where the text mentions 'seven classes of skin lesions' in the context of the confusion matrix analysis, despite the study only classifying four classes.
The dataset contains significant variability in image quality, lighting, and background noise (e.g., other objects in the image), which may introduce bias and reduce real-world robustness.
Performance is uneven across classes, with notably low recall for Melanoma (0.59) and Melanocytic Nevus (0.67), raising concerns about sensitivity for critical cancer diagnoses.
The paper compares YOLOv11 against older Image Processing and ANN methods (72% accuracy) rather than state-of-the-art deep learning baselines, potentially overstating the relative improvement.
No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the internal Kaggle validation set, limiting clinical applicability.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests or confidence intervals for the reported metrics.
Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed, though the use of a public Kaggle dataset with standard splits reduces this risk compared to custom curated datasets.
